title: Chapter 18: Before
content: I can't sleep.

The legal framework is solid. The strategy is sound. Leo mapped five federal violations across Infernal's server architecture, and every one of them is a nail. We have nails. We have a hammer. We have a blueprint for where to swing it.

But something is pulling at me. Not insomnia — I wish it were that simple. This is something lower. Something underneath the thoughts, like a current moving below still water. A need to remember who I'm fighting for.

Not the 847,000. Not the principle. Not even the specific sections of the Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts Regulations that Dante's operation is violating with the elegance of a butcher typing with mittens.
Me. The person I was before the contract.

I get out of bed at 2:47 AM. I don't change out of what I'm wearing. I grab my keys and my phone and I leave the apartment and I walk twenty-two blocks to the place I haven't touched since the deductions started.
My storage unit in Brooklyn.

It's on the fourth floor of a facility that used to be a printing plant. The hallway smells like cardboard dust and industrial cleaner, that specific combination that says: things are stored here, not lived in. The overhead lights flicker — one of them does a slow dying-bulb pulse that makes the corridor feel like a set piece from a horror movie I'd never watch because I'm not a monster.

Unit 4-J. The lock is cold when I touch it. I haven't been here in — I do the math. Seven months. Maybe eight. Long enough for a person to become a different person and then not remember how to become back.
I twist the key. The door rolls up on its track with a sound like a slow exhale.

It's the size of a closet. Eight by ten, maybe. It costs $89 a month — money I used to not have. When I signed the lease I was so proud of myself. A storage unit. Like a real adult with real things worth storing. Now I pay it automatically and never think about it, which is maybe the most depressing sentence I've ever constructed about my own life.
The fluorescent unit light is dead. I use my phone's flashlight.
Inside: my pre-fame life.

Cardboard boxes stacked two high, their sides sagging with the weight of canvases I couldn't sell and couldn't throw away. A black portfolio case leaning against the wall like a drunk at last call. A shoebox — an actual shoebox, Nike, I can't even remember the shoes — full of letters from my grandmother. My old Converse, paint-splattered, the laces tied together because that's how you store shoes when you're seventeen and organizing a closet for the first time. And wedged between the portfolio case and the wall, spine cracked, cover warped with humidity: a sketchbook from my first semester at SVA.
I sit on the concrete floor. The cold bites through my pants immediately. I don't move. I pull the sketchbook into my lap.
Page one.
A self-portrait.

I drew this in my dorm room on a Tuesday night. I know it was Tuesday because I used to draw every Tuesday — a self-imposed deadline, a contract with myself before I ever signed one with anyone else. Charcoal on cheap paper. Unflattering. Honest. Raw.

Dark circles under the eyes so deep they look like bruises. Chapped lips. Hair in a messy bun that's less "effortless" and more "I stopped trying two days ago." The jaw is too sharp, the cheekbones too prominent — I was not eating enough that semester, something the portrait doesn't bother to hide.
But the eyes.
The eyes are alive.

They're full of something my current eyes don't have. Hope? Hunger? Want? It's the look of a person who believes the next thing could be the thing. The big thing. The break. The moment where all the scraping and struggling crystallizes into something with a name and a future and a point.

I stared at myself in a mirror for forty-five minutes and I drew this and I thought: this is who I am. A person with dark circles and chapped lips and eyes that are so full of wanting that it spills out of the charcoal.

Now I look at the portrait and I feel — admiration. Professional admiration. Good use of contrast. Nice economy of line around the jaw. The eyes are a little overworked but the intensity reads. Like a juror at a student show. I'm grading myself.
No warmth. No ache. No flash of recognition that says that's me, that was me, I remember being her.
Just: good composition.
I turn the page.

Sketches of my grandmother's kitchen. The window over the sink, the herbs on the sill in their mismatched terra cotta pots. The way light hit the formica counter at 4 PM — golden, specific, like the sun had an appointment and kept it. I can see the salt shaker shaped like a cat. The drip edge of the window frame where the paint was peeling. The dish towel with the faded strawberries.

I drew this from life. I sat in that kitchen with my sketchbook on my lap and I drew it because I loved it. Not because it was a good composition. Not because the light was interesting. Because it was my grandmother's kitchen and the light hit the counter at 4 PM and she was humming something in the other room and I was alive and she was alive and the world was small in the best way.
I look at it now and I think: nice tonal range on the counter.

The sound of pages turning is the only sound in the unit. That soft friction of paper against paper, like skin against skin, like a whisper that's not quite words. I turn them slowly. Each page is a record. Each page says: this mattered to someone. That someone is occupying my body and she can't feel any of it.
I set the sketchbook aside and reach for the Nike shoebox.

The letters are inside, stacked chronologically because my grandmother would never forgive me for disorder. Her handwriting — shaky, arthritic, determined. The kind of script that was beautiful once and stayed beautiful even when the hands that made it started to fail. She pressed hard. You can feel the grooves.

I pull one from the middle. The envelope is yellowed at the edges. When I open it, I accidentally lick the flap — a reflex from a thousand opened envelopes, muscle memory betraying me — and the taste is disgusting. Old glue. Stale sweet. The ghost of adhesive that gave up years ago. I grimace and pull the letter free.
*Dear Sweetpea,*

*I saw the sunset tonight and thought of you. Remember when you used to call me just to describe the sky? You always saw colors I couldn't. The doctors say my eyes are getting worse but I think it's just that you see more than most people. That's a gift, sweetheart. Don't let anyone tell you it isn't.*

*Love,*
*Grandma*

I read the words. I know this happened. I can fact-check every detail. I used to call her. I used to stand on the fire escape of my second sublet, the one with the broken latch on the window, and I'd watch the sky go orange and pink and purple and I'd call her because the colors were too much to keep inside. She'd put the phone on speaker and set it on the kitchen counter and listen while she cooked, and sometimes she'd say "tell me about the purple" and I'd describe it, specific, like I was mixing paint on my tongue — not lavender, not plum, something between blackberry and bruise, the color at the edge of the sky where the light gives up.
I know this happened. I remember the fire escape. I remember the broken latch. I remember the color between blackberry and bruise.

But the feeling. The specific warmth of holding the phone against my ear and hearing her breathe while I described the sky. The way my chest would get full, not heavy, full — like there was something in there that had weight and the weight was good. The sense that I was sharing beauty with someone who loved me and that the sharing was the whole point, that beauty unshared was only half-alive.
That feeling is gone.
The letter is a photograph of a fire that burned out. You can see the shape of the flames. You can see where the heat was. But you put your hand over the image and the paper is room temperature.
I fold the letter and put it back in the envelope. My hands are steady. That's the worst part. My hands are always steady now.
I'm repacking the shoebox when I see it. A photo, loose, slipped between two letters like a bookmark. I don't remember putting it there.
It's me and Leo.

Leo's arm around my shoulders. We're at Coney Island — I can see the Wonder Wheel in the background, that big slow circle of light that turns against the sky like a wheel of fortune that only lands on good things. I'm laughing. Not smiling. Laughing. The kind of laugh that hurts your face, the kind where your eyes are squeezed shut and your mouth is open too wide and you look unhinged and you don't care because whatever made you laugh has taken over your whole body.

Leo is looking at me, not at the camera. That's what hits first. He's not performing for the photo. He's watching me laugh. His expression is — I can't name it. I have the word. The word is "fond." But fond is something I understand the way I understand German: I can read it, I can translate it, I don't feel it.
I look at my own face. At the laugh. I can see the joy in it the way you can see the sun in a photograph of a sunset. The light is right there. It's documented. It happened.
I can't reach it.

I can see it on my own face and I cannot reach it. It's behind glass. It's on the other side of a membrane I can't break. The joy is right there, documented, proven, and I'm on this side, pressing my palm flat against the surface and feeling nothing.
This is the worst thing that's happened to me in a storage unit that costs $89 a month.
I put the photo down. I sit on the concrete floor and I try to cry.

The tears come out. They're warm and they run down my face and they drip off my chin onto my shirt. But the sob doesn't come. The gasping, the heaving, the thing that opens your chest and lets the pressure out — that doesn't happen. The tears are mechanical. Output without catharsis. Water leaving the body through the wrong exit. Like sweating when you're cold. All the physical signs of release with none of the release.

I sit there on the cold concrete and I let the tears run and I wait for the sob that doesn't come and I understand, finally, what the contract actually took. It didn't take my memories. It took the bridge between memory and feeling. The thing that lets you access the past as something that happened to you instead of something you read about. The contract amputated the ganglion where knowing becomes feeling, and I've been walking around with a phantom limb, reaching for a warmth that isn't there anymore.
The tears stop. I wipe my face with my sleeve. The cardboard dust catches in the wet and I can feel it, small and gritty against my skin. At least I can feel that.
I pack everything back up.

The shoebox goes on top of the canvases. The letters go in the shoebox. The photo of me and Leo goes between the letters where I found it. The Converse stay on the floor. The portfolio case leans against the wall like nothing happened.
I take one thing with me. The sketchbook.

Not because it makes me feel anything. It doesn't. I looked at every page and I felt the professional admiration of a stranger and nothing else. The sketchbook is evidence. Proof that a version of Chloe Hart existed who could feel things — who drew her grandmother's kitchen because she loved it, who called to describe sunsets because sharing beauty was the whole point, who laughed at Coney Island with her whole face. That version is worth fighting for. Not because I can access her. Because she's archived.
And archives can be restored.
I roll down the door. The lock clicks shut. The sound echoes in the empty hallway, off the concrete floor and the corrugated metal doors and the flickering light that still hasn't died.

I walk to the subway. It's 4:12 AM. The platform is empty except for a man sleeping across three seats and a woman reading a paperback with the concentration of someone who will finish this chapter even if the MTA sets the station on fire. The downtown train arrives in four minutes. I stand near the edge and I hold the sketchbook against my chest like it's something warm. It's not. It's room temperature. Everything is room temperature now.
My phone buzzes.
A text. I almost don't check it. What could anyone need at 4 AM that isn't bad news?
I check it.
Evelyn Hart. My mother. The woman who has been not calling me back for two weeks.
**You called?**

Two words. Classic Evelyn Hart. No "Hi." No "How are you." No "I'm sorry I didn't answer the last seventeen times." Just the factual acknowledgment that a call occurred and the implicit question of what I want. Economy of language that I used to think was coldness and now recognize as something more complicated — a woman who keeps all her doors locked and leaves one window cracked, just barely.
I type back. My thumbs are steady. They're always steady.
**I need a lawyer. Can we meet?**
I stare at the screen. The cursor blinks. The message says delivered. Then read. Then nothing.
Three dots appear. She's typing.
The dots disappear. She stopped.
I wait. The train is a distant rumble somewhere in the tunnel.
The dots appear again. Disappear. Appear.
Then:
**Thursday. 2 PM. My office.**

Thursday. Two in the afternoon. Her office. Not dinner. Not coffee. The conference room, the professional setting, the neutral ground where Evelyn Hart is most herself — behind a desk with the skyline behind her, every word calibrated, every emotion filed under "privileged."
But she said yes.
The mother who couldn't say "I love you" could say "My office." The mother who let three weeks of calls go to voicemail could find five minutes in a Thursday afternoon.
That's the window. Cracked, barely, but open.

The train arrives. I step on. I sit in a seat by the door and I hold the sketchbook and I don't feel the weight of it but I know the weight is there. The Before is gone. But it's not destroyed. It's archived in a unit that costs $89 a month, in a sketchbook from my first semester, in letters from a woman who thought I was a gift, in a photo of a laugh I can't reach.
Archives can be restored.
If you fight hard enough.
